GO Term Overlap Analysis

Ingo Müller

2025-12-16

GO-term venn diagrams

Explore the overlap between different functional annotation methods

Initial setup

Clear workspace and load the necessary packages.

rm(list = ls())

# Load required packages
library(tidyverse)
library(ggVennDiagram)
library(grid)
library(gridExtra)

# Set working directory!
PATH <- "/home/ingomue/1.Work/1.Projects/2025_Aquarius/06.func_annot/"
setwd(PATH)

Loading and preprocess data

Load each tsv and make lists of unique go terms and genes Also put each feature together with its associated GO-terms and genes to see how much those overlap diamond. By features, I refer to anything that was annotated by the structural annotation by braker, most commonly these will be transcripts and CDS (eggnog only annotates CDS), e.g. in the gff they will be labelled as g23.t1 (gene 23, transcript 1).

Also an important note is that the interpro+BLAST annotation builds on top of the interpro annotation, so I expect significant overlap and no exclusive GO-terms/genes in the interpro annotation.

# EggNOG with DIAMOND alignment
egg_diamond <- read.table(paste0(PATH, "01.input/eggnog_diamond.tsv"),
                          header = T,sep = "\t", quote = "")

# 1. All unique genes (excluding NA)
egg_dia_genes <- unique(egg_diamond$GENE[!is.na(egg_diamond$GENE)])

# 2. All unique GO terms (excluding NA)
egg_diamond_uniq <- egg_diamond %>%
  filter(!is.na(GO)) %>%
  separate_rows(GO, sep = ",") %>%
  mutate(GO = str_trim(GO)) %>%
  pull(GO) %>%
  unique()

# 3. Features + genes
egg_diamond_featgen <- egg_diamond %>%
  filter(!is.na(GENE)) %>%
  mutate(feat_gene = paste(seqid, GENE, sep = "_")) %>%
  pull(feat_gene)

# 4. Features + GO terms
egg_diamond_featGO <- egg_diamond %>%
  filter(!is.na(GO)) %>%
  separate_rows(GO, sep = ",") %>%
  mutate(GO = str_trim(GO),
         feat_GO = paste(seqid, GO, sep = "_")) %>%
  pull(feat_GO)

# EggNOG with mmseqs2 on default mode
egg_mm_def <- read.table(paste0(PATH, "01.input/eggnog_mm_def.tsv"),
                         header = T,sep = "\t", quote = "")

egg_mm_def_genes <- unique(egg_mm_def$GENE[!is.na(egg_mm_def$GENE)])

egg_mm_def_uniq <- egg_mm_def %>%
  filter(!is.na(GO)) %>%
  separate_rows(GO, sep = ",") %>%
  mutate(GO = str_trim(GO)) %>%
  pull(GO) %>%
  unique()

egg_mm_def_featgen <- egg_mm_def %>%
  filter(!is.na(GENE)) %>%
  mutate(feat_gene = paste(seqid, GENE, sep = "_")) %>%
  pull(feat_gene)

egg_mm_def_featGO <- egg_mm_def %>%
  filter(!is.na(GO)) %>%
  separate_rows(GO, sep = ",") %>%
  mutate(GO = str_trim(GO),
         feat_GO = paste(seqid, GO, sep = "_")) %>%
  pull(feat_GO)

# EggNOG with mmseqs2 on high-sensitivity mode
egg_mm_HC <- read.table(paste0(PATH, "01.input/eggnog_mm_HS.tsv"),
                         header = T,sep = "\t", quote = "")

egg_mm_HC_genes <- unique(egg_mm_HC$GENE[!is.na(egg_mm_HC$GENE)])

egg_mm_HC_uniq <- egg_mm_HC %>%
  filter(!is.na(GO)) %>%
  separate_rows(GO, sep = ",") %>%
  mutate(GO = str_trim(GO)) %>%
  pull(GO) %>%
  unique()

egg_mm_HC_featgen <- egg_mm_HC %>%
  filter(!is.na(GENE)) %>%
  mutate(feat_gene = paste(seqid, GENE, sep = "_")) %>%
  pull(feat_gene)

egg_mm_HC_featGO <- egg_mm_HC %>%
  filter(!is.na(GO)) %>%
  separate_rows(GO, sep = ",") %>%
  mutate(GO = str_trim(GO),
         feat_GO = paste(seqid, GO, sep = "_")) %>%
  pull(feat_GO)

# InterProScan
interpro <- read.table(paste0(PATH, "01.input/interpro.tsv"),
                  header = T, sep = "\t", quote = "")

interpro_genes <- unique(interpro$GENE[!is.na(interpro$GENE)])

interpro_uniq <- interpro %>%
  filter(!is.na(GO)) %>%
  separate_rows(GO, sep = ",") %>%
  mutate(GO = str_trim(GO)) %>%
  pull(GO) %>%
  unique()

interpro_featgen <- interpro %>%
  filter(!is.na(GENE)) %>%
  mutate(feat_gene = paste(seqid, GENE, sep = "_")) %>%
  pull(feat_gene)

interpro_featGO <- interpro %>%
  filter(!is.na(GO)) %>%
  separate_rows(GO, sep = ",") %>%
  mutate(GO = str_trim(GO),
         feat_GO = paste(seqid, GO, sep = "_")) %>%
  pull(feat_GO)
  
# InterProScan + BLAST
interpro_blast <- read.table(paste0(PATH, "01.input/interpro_blast.tsv"),
                       header = T, sep = "\t", quote = "")

interpro_blast_genes <- unique(interpro_blast$GENE[!is.na(interpro_blast$GENE)])

interpro_blast_uniq <- interpro_blast %>%
  filter(!is.na(GO)) %>%
  separate_rows(GO, sep = ",") %>%
  mutate(GO = str_trim(GO)) %>%
  pull(GO) %>%
  unique()

interpro_blast_featgen <- interpro_blast %>%
  filter(!is.na(GENE)) %>%
  mutate(feat_gene = paste(seqid, GENE, sep = "_")) %>%
  pull(feat_gene)

interpro_blast_featGO <- interpro_blast %>%
  filter(!is.na(GO)) %>%
  separate_rows(GO, sep = ",") %>%
  mutate(GO = str_trim(GO),
         feat_GO = paste(seqid, GO, sep = "_")) %>%
  pull(feat_GO)

Venn Diagrams

Visualize how these different methods overlap. The firebrick color gradient shows the degree of overlap: darker red means more shared GO-terms/genes.

1) EggNOG with mmseqs2 default vs high-sensitivity modes

# Create all 4 plots
p1 <- ggVennDiagram(list(MMDEF=egg_mm_def_uniq, MMHC=egg_mm_HC_uniq), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Unique GO Terms")

p2 <- ggVennDiagram(list(MMDEF=egg_mm_def_featGO, MMHC=egg_mm_HC_featGO), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Feature+GO Combinations")

p3 <- ggVennDiagram(list(MMDEF=egg_mm_def_genes, MMHC=egg_mm_HC_genes), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Unique Genes")

p4 <- ggVennDiagram(list(MMDEF=egg_mm_def_featgen, MMHC=egg_mm_HC_featgen), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Feature+Gene Combinations")

# Arrange in 2x2 grid
grid.arrange(p1, p2, p3, p4, ncol = 2, 
             top = textGrob("EggNOG: mmseqs2 Default vs High-Sensitivity", gp=gpar(fontsize=25)))

In terms of GO-terms these are pretty much identical. Perhaps the high-sensitivity algorithm is able to annotate slightly more GO-terms on individual features than default values (5596 vs 2854 [out of a total of 802 128 feature+GO combinations])

2) EggNOG mmseqs2 default, high-sensitivity and Diamond

p1 <- ggVennDiagram(list(DIA=egg_diamond_uniq, MMDEF=egg_mm_def_uniq, MMHC=egg_mm_HC_uniq), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Unique GO Terms")

p2 <- ggVennDiagram(list(DIA=egg_diamond_featGO, MMDEF=egg_mm_def_featGO, MMHC=egg_mm_HC_featGO), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Feature+GO Combinations")

p3 <- ggVennDiagram(list(DIA=egg_dia_genes, MMDEF=egg_mm_def_genes, MMHC=egg_mm_HC_genes), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Unique Genes")

p4 <- ggVennDiagram(list(DIA=egg_diamond_featgen, MMDEF=egg_mm_def_featgen, MMHC=egg_mm_HC_featgen), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Feature+Gene Combinations")

grid.arrange(p1, p2, p3, p4, ncol = 2,
             top = textGrob("EggNOG: Diamond vs mmseqs2 Default vs High-Sensitivity", gp=gpar(fontsize=25)))

Diamond results seem to be almost same as mmseqs2, but it may be slightly better as it picks up a few more GO-terms and genes (unique and per-feature).

3) InterProScan vs InterProScan+BLAST

p1 <- ggVennDiagram(list(IPS=interpro_uniq, IPSBL=interpro_blast_uniq), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Unique GO Terms")

p2 <- ggVennDiagram(list(IPS=interpro_featGO, IPSBL=interpro_blast_featGO), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Feature+GO Combinations")

p3 <- ggVennDiagram(list(IPS=interpro_genes, IPSBL=interpro_blast_genes), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Unique Genes")

p4 <- ggVennDiagram(list(IPS=interpro_featgen, IPSBL=interpro_blast_featgen), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Feature+Gene Combinations")

grid.arrange(p1, p2, p3, p4, ncol = 2,
             top = textGrob("InterProScan vs InterProScan+BLAST", gp=gpar(fontsize=25)))

As expected, there are no exclusive features to interproscan as this annotation is also included in the BLAST+Interpro annotation. This mainly serves to quantify how much more information is added by BLAST. BLAST itself doesn’t add information on GO-terms, so it only adds several new gene annotations.

4) mmseqs2, Diamond and InterProScan

I am including mmseqs2 only with high-sensitivity settings in this comparison as it had (only few) more annotated features than its default settings. First, without BLAST results, in case I see higher overlap between interproscan on its own and the eggnog approaches.

p1 <- ggVennDiagram(list(DIA=egg_diamond_uniq, MMHC=egg_mm_HC_uniq, IPS=interpro_uniq), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Unique GO Terms")

p2 <- ggVennDiagram(list(DIA=egg_diamond_featGO, MMHC=egg_mm_HC_featGO, IPS=interpro_featGO), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Feature+GO Combinations")

p3 <- ggVennDiagram(list(DIA=egg_dia_genes, MMHC=egg_mm_HC_genes, IPS=interpro_genes), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Unique Genes")

p4 <- ggVennDiagram(list(DIA=egg_diamond_featgen, MMHC=egg_mm_HC_featgen, IPS=interpro_featgen), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Feature+Gene Combinations")

grid.arrange(p1, p2, p3, p4, ncol = 2,
             top = textGrob("Diamond + mmseqs2 High-Sensitivity + InterProScan", gp=gpar(fontsize=25)))

Highest overlap between the eggnog algorithms. But also very high proportion of gene annotations exclusive to interproscan.

5) mmseqs2, Diamond and InterProScan+BLAST

p1 <- ggVennDiagram(list(DIA=egg_diamond_uniq, MMHC=egg_mm_HC_uniq, IPSBL=interpro_blast_uniq), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Unique GO Terms")

p2 <- ggVennDiagram(list(DIA=egg_diamond_featGO, MMHC=egg_mm_HC_featGO, IPSBL=interpro_blast_featGO), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Feature+GO Combinations")

p3 <- ggVennDiagram(list(DIA=egg_dia_genes, MMHC=egg_mm_HC_genes, IPSBL=interpro_blast_genes), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Unique Genes")

p4 <- ggVennDiagram(list(DIA=egg_diamond_featgen, MMHC=egg_mm_HC_featgen, IPSBL=interpro_blast_featgen), label_alpha = 0) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="white", high="firebrick") +
  ggtitle("Feature+Gene Combinations")

grid.arrange(p1, p2, p3, p4, ncol = 2,
             top = textGrob("Diamond + mmseqs2 High-Sensitivity + InterProScan+BLAST", gp=gpar(fontsize=25)))

Similar picture as just with interproscan, but BLAST with its additional gene annotations increases the fraction of genes annotated exclusively by interproscan+BLAST even further.

Summary

  • mmseqs2 settings don’t make as big of a difference as I expected
  • Diamond seems to have a few more features than mmseqs2, but there are still a few exclusive annotations found by mmseqs2
  • InterProScan adds many more genes terms not found by EggNOG, and adds a smaller fraction (5%) of additional GO-terms
  • BLAST adds even more annotated genes

I will definitely merge the annotation of eggnog (Diamond) and interproscan+BLAST, as mmseqs2 still detects a few additional features, I would also add these on top in order to get a more complete picture.

Next step: BUSCO scores for each annotation.
